title: NeuS: Learning Neural Implicit Surfaces by Volume Rendering for Multi-view Reconstruction [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## **NeuS: Learning Neural Implicit Surfaces by Volume Rendering for Multi-view Reconstruction**

**Peng Wang** _[†]_ **, Lingjie Liu** _[‡∗]_ **, Yuan Liu** _[†]_ **, Christian Theobalt** _[‡]_ **, Taku Komura** _[†]_ **, Wenping Wang** _[⋄∗] †_ The University of Hong Kong _‡_ Max Planck Institute for Informatics _⋄_ Texas A&M University

> _†_ `{pwang3,yliu,taku}@cs.hku.hk` _‡_ `{lliu,theobalt}@mpi-inf.mpg.de`

> _⋄_ `wenping@tamu.edu`

## **Abstract**

We present a novel neural surface reconstruction method, called _NeuS_ , for reconstructing objects and scenes with high fidelity from 2D image inputs. Existing neural surface reconstruction approaches, such as DVR [Niemeyer et al., 2020] and IDR [Yariv et al., 2020], require foreground mask as supervision, easily get trapped in local minima, and therefore struggle with the reconstruction of objects with severe self-occlusion or thin structures. Meanwhile, recent neural methods for novel view synthesis, such as NeRF [Mildenhall et al., 2020] and its variants, use volume rendering to produce a neural scene representation with robustness of optimization, even for highly complex objects. However, extracting high-quality surfaces from this learned implicit representation is difficult because there are not sufficient surface constraints in the representation. In NeuS, we propose to represent a surface as the zero-level set of a _signed distance function_ (SDF) and develop a new volume rendering method to train a neural SDF representation. We observe that the conventional volume rendering method causes inherent geometric errors (i.e. bias) for surface reconstruction, and therefore propose a new formulation that is free of bias in the first order of approximation, thus leading to more accurate surface reconstruction even without the mask supervision. Experiments on the DTU dataset and the BlendedMVS dataset show that NeuS outperforms the state-of-the-arts in high-quality surface reconstruction, especially for objects and scenes with complex structures and self-occlusion.

## **1 Introduction**

Reconstructing surfaces from multi-view images is a fundamental problem in computer vision and computer graphics. 3D reconstruction with neural implicit representations has recently become a highly promising alternative to classical reconstruction approaches [35, 8, 2] due to its high reconstruction quality and its potential to reconstruct complex objects that are difficult for classical approaches, such as non-Lambertian surfaces and thin structures. Recent works represent surfaces as signed distance functions (SDF) [46, 49, 17, 22] or occupancy [29, 30]. To train their neural models, these methods use a differentiable surface rendering method to render a 3D object into images and compare them against input images for supervision. For example, IDR [46] produces impressive reconstruction results, but it fails to reconstruct objects with complex structures that causes abrupt depth changes. The cause of this limitation is that the surface rendering method used in IDR only considers a single surface intersection point for each ray. Consequently, the gradient only exists at this single point, which is too local for effective back propagation and would get optimization stuck in a poor local minimum when there are abrupt changes of depth on images. Furthermore, object

- _∗_ Corresponding authors.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

masks are needed as supervision for converging to a valid surface. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a) top, with the radical depth change caused by the hole, the neural network would incorrectly predict the points near the front surface to be blue, failing to find the far-back blue surface. The actual test example in Fig. 1 (b) shows that IDR fails to correctly reconstruct the surfaces near the edges with abrupt depth changes.

Recently, NeRF [28] and its variants have explored to use a volume rendering method to learn a volumetric radiance field for novel view synthesis. This volume rendering approach samples multiple points along each ray and perform _α_ -composition of the colors of the sampled points to produce the output pixel colors for training purposes. The advantage of the volume rendering approach is that it can handle abrupt depth changes, because it considers multiple points along the ray and so all the sample points, either near the surface or on the far surface, produce gradient signals for back propagation. For example, referring Fig. 1 (a) bottom, when the near surface (yellow) is found to have inconsistent colors with the input image, the volume rendering approach is capable of training the network to find the far-back surface to produce the correct scene representation. However, since it is intended for novel view synthesis rather than surface reconstruction, NeRF only learns a volume density field, from which it is difficult to extract a high-quality surface. Fig. 1 (b) shows a surface extracted as a level-set surface of the density field learned by NeRF. Although the surface correctly accounts for abrupt depth changes, it contains conspicuous noise in some planar regions.

Figure 1: (a) Illustration of the surface rendering and volume rendering. (b) A toy example of bamboo planter, where there are occlusions on the top of the planter. Compared to the state-of-the-art methods, our approach can handle the occlusions and achieve better reconstruction quality.

In this work, we present a new neural rendering scheme, called _NeuS_ , for multi-view surface reconstruction. NeuS uses the _signed distance function_ (SDF) for surface representation and uses a novel volume rendering scheme to learn a neural SDF representation. Specifically, by introducing a density distribution induced by SDF, we make it possible to apply the volume rendering approach to learning an implicit SDF representation and thus have the best of both worlds, i.e. an accurate surface representation using a neural SDF model and robust network training in the presence of abrupt depth changes as enabled by volume rendering. Note that simply applying a standard volume rendering method to the density associated with SDF would lead to discernible bias (i.e. inherent geometric errors) in the reconstructed surfaces. This is a new and important observation that we will elaborate later. Therefore we propose a novel volume rendering scheme to ensure unbiased surface reconstruction in the first-order approximation of SDF. Experiments on both DTU dataset and BlendedMVS dataset demonstrated that NeuS is capable of reconstructing complex 3D objects and scenes with severe occlusions and delicate structures, even without foreground masks as supervision. It outperforms the state-of-the-art neural scene representation methods, namely IDR [46] and NeRF [28], in terms of reconstruction quality.

## **2 Related Works**

**Classical Multi-view Surface and Volumetric Reconstruction.** Traditional multi-view 3D reconstruction methods can be roughly classified into two categories: point- and surface-based reconstruction [2, 8, 9, 35] and volumetric reconstruction [6, 3, 36]. Point- and surface-based reconstruction methods estimate the depth map of each pixel by exploiting inter-image photometric consistency [8] and then fuse the depth maps into a global dense point cloud [25, 48]. The surface reconstruction is usually done as a post processing with methods like screened Poisson surface reconstruction [16]. The

2

reconstruction quality heavily relies on the quality of correspondence matching, and the difficulties in matching correspondence for objects without rich textures often lead to severe artifacts and missing parts in the reconstruction results. Alternatively, volumetric reconstruction methods circumvent the difficulty of explicit correspondence matching by estimating occupancy and color in a voxel grid from multi-view images and evaluating the color consistency of each voxel. Due to limited achievable voxel resolution, these methods cannot achieve high accuracy.

**Neural Implicit Representation.** Some methods enforce 3D understanding in a deep learning framework by introducing inductive biases. These inductive biases can be explicit representations, such as voxel grids [13, 5, 44], point cloud [7, 24, 18], meshes [41, 43, 14], and implicit representations. The implicit representations encoded by a neural network has gained a lot of attention recently, since it is continuous and can achieve high spatial resolution. This representation has been applied successfully to shape representation [26, 27, 31, 4, 1, 10, 47, 32], novel view synthesis [38, 23, 15, 28, 21, 33, 34, 40, 37] and multi-view 3D reconstruction [46, 29, 17, 12, 22].

Our work mainly focuses on learning implicit neural representation encoding both geometry and appearance in 3D space from 2D images via classical rendering techniques. Limited in this scope, the related works can be roughly categorized based on the rendering techniques used, i.e. surface rendering based methods and volume rendering based methods. Surface rendering based methods [29, 17, 46, 22] assume that the color of ray only relies on the color of an intersection of the ray with the scene geometry, which makes the gradient only backpropagated to a local region near the intersection. Therefore, such methods struggle with reconstructing complex objects with severe self-occlusions and sudden depth changes. Furthermore, they usually require object masks as supervision. On the contrary, our method performs well for such challenging cases without the need of masks.

Volume rendering based methods, such as NeRF[28], render an image by _α_ -compositing colors of the sampled points along each ray. As explained in the introduction, it can handle sudden depth changes and synthesize high-quality images. However, extracting high-fidelity surface from the learned implicit field is difficult because the density-based scene representation lacks sufficient constraints on its level sets. In contrast, our method combines the advantages of surface rendering based and volume rendering based methods by constraining the scene space as a signed distance function but applying volume rendering to train this representation with robustness. UNISURF [30], a concurrent work, also learns an implicit surface via volume rendering. It improves the reconstruction quality by shrinking the sample region of volume rendering during the optimization. Our method differs from UNISURF in that UNISURF represents the surface by occupancy values, while our method represents the scene by an SDF and thus can naturally extract the surface as the zero-level set of it, yielding better reconstruction accuracy than UNISURF, as will be seen later in the experiment section.

## **3 Method**

Given a set of posed images _{Ik}_ of a 3D object, our goal is to reconstruct the surface _S_ of it. The surface is represented by the zero-level set of a neural implicit SDF. In order to learn the weights of the neural network, we developed a novel volume rendering method to render images from the implicit SDF and minimize the difference between the rendered images and the input images. This volume rendering approach ensures robust optimization in NeuS for reconstructing objects of complex structures.

## **3.1 Rendering Procedure**

**Scene representation.** With NeuS, the scene of an object to be reconstructed is represented by two functions: _f_ : R[3] _→_ R that maps a spatial position **x** _∈_ R[3] to its signed distance to the object, and _c_ : R[3] _×_ S[2] _→_ R[3] that encodes the color associated with a point **x** _∈_ R[3] and a viewing direction **v** _∈_ S[2] . Both functions are encoded by Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLP). The surface _S_ of the object is represented by the zero-level set of its SDF, that is,

In order to apply a volume rendering method to training the SDF network, we first introduce a probability density function _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )), called _S-density_ , where _f_ ( **x** ), **x** _∈_ R[3] , is the signed distance

3

function and _φs_ ( _x_ ) = _se[−][sx] /_ (1 + _e[−][sx]_ )[2] , commonly known as the _logistic density distribution_ , is the derivative of the Sigmoid function Φ _s_ ( _x_ ) = (1 + _e[−][sx]_ ) _[−]_[1] , i.e., _φs_ ( _x_ ) = Φ _[′] s_[(] _[x]_[)][.][In principle] _φs_ ( _x_ ) can be any unimodal (i.e. bell-shaped) density distribution centered at 0; here we choose the logistic density distribution for its computational convenience. Note that the standard deviation of _φs_ ( _x_ ) is given by 1 _/s_ , which is also a trainable parameter, that is, 1 _/s_ approaches to zero as the network training converges.

Intuitively, the main idea of NeuS is that, with the aid of the S-density field _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )), volume rendering is used to train the SDF network with only 2D input images as supervision. Upon successful minimization of a loss function based on this supervision, the zero-level set of the network-encoded SDF is expected to represent an accurately reconstructed surface _S_ , with its induced S-density _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )) assuming prominently high values near the surface.

**Rendering.** To learn the parameters of the neural SDF and color field, we advise a volume rendering scheme to render images from the proposed SDF representation. Given a pixel, we denote the ray emitted from this pixel as _{_ **p** ( _t_ ) = **o** + _t_ **v** _|t ≥_ 0 _}_ , where **o** is the center of the camera and **v** is the unit direction vector of the ray. We accumulate the colors along the ray by

where _C_ ( **o** _,_ **v** ) is the output color for this pixel, _w_ ( _t_ ) a weight for the point **p** ( _t_ ), and _c_ ( **p** ( _t_ ) _,_ **v** ) the color at the point **p** along the viewing direction **v** .

**Requirements on weight function** . The key to learn an accurate SDF representation from 2D images is to build an appropriate connection between output colors and SDF, i.e., to derive an appropriate weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) on the ray based on the SDF _f_ of the scene. In the following, we list the requirements on the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ).

1. **Unbiased** . Given a camera ray **p** ( _t_ ), _w_ ( _t_ ) attains a locally maximal value at a surface intersection point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ), i.e. with _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0, that is, the point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ) is on the zero-level set of the SDF ( **x** ).

2. **Occlusion-aware** . Given any two depth values _t_ 0 and _t_ 1 satisfying _f_ ( _t_ 0) = _f_ ( _t_ 1), _w_ ( _t_ 0) _>_ 0, _w_ ( _t_ 1) _>_ 0, and _t_ 0 _< t_ 1, there is _w_ ( _t_ 0) _> w_ ( _t_ 1). That is, when two points have the same SDF value (thus the same SDF-induced S-density value), the point nearer to the view point should have a larger contribution to the final output color than does the other point.

An unbiased weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) guarantees that the intersection of the camera ray with the zero-level set of SDF contributes most to the pixel color. The occlusion-aware property ensures that when a ray sequentially passes multiple surfaces, the rendering procedure will correctly use the color of the surface nearest to the camera to compute the output color.

Next, we will first introduce a naive way of defining the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) that directly using the standard pipeline of volume rendering, and explain why it is not appropriate for reconstruction before introducing our novel construction of _w_ ( _t_ ).

dard volume density formulation [28], here _σ_ ( _t_ ) is set to be equal to the S-density value, i.e. _σ_ ( _t_ ) = _φs_ ( _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ ))) and the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) is computed by Eqn. 3. Although the resulting weight function is occlusion-aware, it is biased as it introduces inherent errors in the reconstructed surfaces. As illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) attains a local maximum at a point before the ray reaches the surface point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ), satisfying _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0. This fact will be proved in the supplementary material.
**Our solution** . To introduce our solution, we first introduce a straightforward way to construct an unbiased weight function, which directly uses the normalized S-density as weights

This construction of weight function is unbiased, but not occlusion-aware. For example, if the ray penetrates two surfaces, the SDF function _f_ will have two zero points on the ray, which leads to two peaks on the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) and the resulting weight function will equally blend the colors of two surfaces without considering occlusions.

To this end, now we shall design the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) that is both occlusion-aware and unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF, based on the aforementioned straightforward construction. To ensure an occlusion-aware property of the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ), we will still follow the basic framework of volume rendering as Eqn. 3. However, different from the conventional treatment as in naive solution above, we define our function _w_ ( _t_ ) from the S-density in a new manner. We first define an opaque density function _ρ_ ( _t_ ), which is the counterpart of the volume density _σ_ in standard volume rendering. Then we compute the new weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) by

**How we derive opaque density** _ρ_ . We will first consider a simple case where there is only one surface intersection, and the surface is simply a plane. Since Eqn. 4 indeed satisfies the above requirements under this assumption, we derive the underlying opaque density _ρ_ corresponding to the weight definition of Eqn. 4 using the framework of volume rendering. Then we will generalize this opaque density to the general case of multiple surface intersections.

Specifically, in the simple case of a single plane intersection, it is easy to see that the signed distance function _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )) is _−|_ cos( _θ_ ) _| ·_ ( _t − t[∗]_ ), where _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0, and _θ_ is the angle between the view direction **v** and the outward surface normal vector **n** . Because the surface is assumed locally, _|_ cos( _θ_ ) _|_ is a constant. It follows from Eqn. 4 that

Based on this equation, the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) can be computed with standard volume rendering as in Eqn. 5. The illustration in the case of multiple surface intersection is shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Illustration of weight distribution in case of multiple surface intersection.

The following theorem states that in general cases (i.e.,

including both single surface intersection and multiple surface intersections) the weight function defined by Eqn. 10 and Eqn. 5 is unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF. The proof is given in the supplementary material.

**Theorem 1** _Suppose that a smooth surface_ S _is defined by the zero-level set of the signed distance function f_ ( **x** ) = 0 _, and a ray_ **p** ( _t_ ) = **o** + _t_ **v** _enters the surface_ S _from outside to inside, with the intersection point at_ **p** ( _t[∗]_ ) _, that is, f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0 _and there exists an interval_ [ _tl, tr_ ] _such that t[∗] ∈_ [ _tl, tr_ ] _and f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )) _is monotonically decreasing in_ [ _tl, tr_ ] _. Suppose that in this local interval_ [ _tl, tr_ ] _, the surface can be tangentially approximated by a sufficiently small planar patch, i.e., ∇_ **f** _is regarded as fixed. Then, the weight function w_ ( _t_ ) _computed by Eqn. 10 and Eqn. 5 in_ [ _tl, tr_ ] _attains its maximum at t[∗] ._

## **3.2 Training**

To train NeuS, we minimize the difference between the rendered colors and the ground truth colors, without any 3D supervision. Besides colors, we can also utilize the masks for supervision if provided.

Specifically, we optimize our neural networks and inverse standard deviation _s_ by randomly sampling a batch of pixels and their corresponding rays in world space _P_ = _{Ck, Mk,_ **o** _k,_ **v** _k}_ , where _Ck_ is its pixel color and _Mk ∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ is its optional mask value, from an image in every iteration. We assume the point sampling size is _n_ and the batch size is _m_ . The loss function is defined as

The color loss _Lcolor_ is defined as

Same as IDR[46], we empirically choose _R_ as L1 loss, which in our observation is robust to outliers and stable in training.

We add an Eikonal term [10] on the sampled points to regularize the SDF of _fθ_ by

The optional mask loss _Lmask_ is defined as
> where _O_[ˆ] _k_ =[�] _[n] i_ =1 _[T][k,i][α][k,i]_[is the sum of weights along the camera ray, and][ BCE][ is the binary cross] entropy loss.

**Hierarchical sampling** . In this work, we follow a similar hierarchical sampling strategy as in NeRF [28]. We first uniformly sample the points on the ray and then iteratively conduct importance sampling on top of the coarse probability estimation. The difference is that, unlike NeRF which simultaneously optimizes a coarse network and a fine network, we only maintain one network, where the probability in coarse sampling is computed based on the S-density _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )) with fixed standard deviations while the probability of fine sampling is computed based on _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )) with the learned _s_ . Details of hierarchical sampling strategy are provided in supplementary materials.

## **4 Experiments**

## **4.1 Experimental settings**

**Datasets.** To evaluate our approach and baseline methods, we use 15 scenes from the DTU dataset [11], same as those used in IDR [46], with a wide variety of materials, appearance and geometry, including challenging cases for reconstruction algorithms, such as non-Lambertian surfaces and thin structures. Each scene contains 49 or 64 images with the image resolution of 1600 _×_ 1200. Each scene was tested with and without foreground masks provided by IDR [46]. We further tested on 7 challenging scenes from the low-res set of the BlendedMVS dataset [45](CC-4 License). Each scene has 31 _−_ 143 images at 768 _×_ 576 pixels and masks are provided by the BlendedMVS dataset. We further captured two thin objects with 32 input images to test our approach on thin structure reconstruction.

**Baselines.** (1) The state-of-the-art surface rendering approach – IDR [46]: IDR can reconstruct surface with high quality but requires foreground masks as supervision; Since IDR has demonstrated superior quality compared to another surface rendering based method – DVR [29], we did not conduct a comparison with DVR. (2) The state-of-the-art volume rendering approach – NeRF [28]: We use a threshold of 25 to extract mesh from the learned density field. We validate this choice in the supplementary material. (3) A widely-used classical MVS method – COLMAP [35]: We reconstruct a mesh from the output point cloud of COLMAP with Screened Poisson Surface Reconstruction [16]. (4) The concurrent work which unifies surface rendering and volume rendering with an occupancy field as scene representation – UNISURF [30]. More details of the baseline methods are included in the supplementary material.

7

**Implementation details.** We assume the region of interest is inside a unit sphere. We sample 512 rays per batch and train our model for 300k iterations for 14 hours (for the ‘w/ mask’ setting) and 16 hours (for the ‘w/o mask’ setting) on a single NVIDIA RTX2080Ti GPU. For the ‘w/o mask’ setting, we model the background by NeRF++ [50]. Our network architecture and initialization scheme are similar to those of IDR [46]. More details of the network architecture and training parameters can be found in the supplementary material.

Table 1: Quantitative evaluation on DTU dataset. COLMAP results are achieved by trim=0.

We conducted the comparisons in two settings,

with mask supervision (w/ mask) and without mask supervision (w/o mask). We measure the reconstruction quality with the Chamfer distances in the same way as UNISURF [30] and IDR [46] and report the scores in Table 1. The results show that our approach outperforms the baseline methods on the DTU dataset in both settings – w/ and w/o mask in terms of the Chamfer distance. Note that the reported scores of IDR in the setting of w/ mask and NeRF and UNISURF in the w/o mask setting are from IDR [46] and UNISURF [30].

We conduct the qualitative comparisons on the DTU dataset and the BlendedMVS dataset in both settings, w/ mask and w/o mask, in Figure 4 and Figure 5, respectively. As shown in Figure 4 for the setting of w/ mask, IDR shows limited performance for reconstructing thin metals parts in Scan 37 (DTU), and fails to handle sudden depth changes in Stone (BlendedMVS) due to the local optimization process in surface rendering. The extracted meshes of NeRF are noisy since the volume density field has not sufficient constraint on its 3D geometry. Regarding the w/o mask setting, we visually compare our method with NeRF and COLMAP in the setting of w/o mask in Figure 5, which shows our reconstructed surfaces are with more fidelity than baselines. with UNISURF [30] on two examples in the w/o mask setting. Note that we use the qualitative results of UNISURF reported their paper for comparison. Our method works better for the objects with abrupt depth changes. More qualitative images are included in the supplementary material.

## **4.3 Analysis**

## **Ablation study.**

To evaluate the effect of the weight calculation, we test three different kinds of weight constructions described in Sec. 3.1: (a) Naive Solution. (b) Straightforward Construction as shown in Eqn. 4. (e) Full Model. As shown in Figure 6, the quantitative result of naive solution is worse than our weight choice (e) in terms of the Chamfer distance. This is because it introduces a bias to the surface reconstruction. If direct construction is used, there are severe artifacts.

**----- End of picture text -----**
Figure 6: Ablation studies. We show the qualitative results and report the quantitative metrics in Chamfer distance and MAE (mean absolute error) between the ground-truth and predicted SDF values.

9

We also studied the effect of Eikonal regularization [10] and geometric initialization [1]. Without Eikonal regularization or geometric initialization, the result on Chamfer distance is on par with that of the full model. However, neither of them can correctly output a signed distance function. This is indicated by the MAE(mean absolute error) between the SDF predictions and corresponding ground-truth SDF, as shown in the bottom line of Figure 6. The MAE is computed on uniformly-sampled points in the object’s bounding sphere. Qualitative results of Figure 7: SDF predictions are provided in the supplementary material.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

Reference Image Ours UNISURF
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 7: Visual comparisons with UNISURF.

**Thin structures.** We additionally show results on two challenging thin objects with 32 input images. The plane with rich texture under the object is used for camera calibration. As shown in Fig. 8, our method is able to accurately reconstruct these thin structures, especially on the edges with abrupt depth changes. Furthermore, different from the methods [39, 19, 42, 20] which only target at high-quality thin structure reconstruction, our method can handle the scenes which have a mixture of thin structures and general objects.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

Reference Image (a) Ours (b) COLMAP𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑚= 10 (c) COLMAP𝑡𝑟𝑖𝑚= 7
**----- End of picture text -----**
Figure 8: Comparison on scenes with thin structure objects. Left half is the depth map while right half is the reconstructed surface.

## **5 Conclusion**

We have proposed _NeuS_ , a new approach to multiview surface reconstruction that represents 3D surfaces as neural SDF and developed a new volume rendering method for training the implicit SDF representation. NeuS produces high-quality reconstruction and successfully reconstructs objects with severe occlusions and complex structures. It outperforms the state-of-the-arts both qualitatively and quantitatively. One limitation of our method is that although our method does not heavily rely on correspondence matching of texture features, the performance would still degrade for textureless objects (we show the failure cases in the supplementary material). Moreover, NeuS has only a single scale parameter _s_ that is used to model the standard deviation of the probability distribution for all the spatial location. Hence, an interesting future research topic is to model the probability with different variances for different spatial locations together with the optimization of scene representation, depending on different local geometric characteristics. Negative societal impact: like many other learning-based works, our method requires a large amount of computational resources for network training, which can be a concern for global climate change.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents NeuS, a neural implicit surface reconstruction method that elegantly bridges two dominant paradigms in the field: surface rendering (e.g., IDR) and volume rendering (e.g., NeRF). The core contribution—representing surfaces as the zero-level set of an SDF while using a carefully designed volume rendering scheme that is unbiased to first-order approximation—is well-motivated and addresses a genuine and important limitation of prior work, namely the inability of surface-rendering methods to handle abrupt depth changes and the difficulty of extracting clean geometry from NeRF-style density fields. The introduction is clearly written, with Fig. 1 providing an intuitive illustration of why gradient locality hampers IDR on self-occluded regions, and the claimed elimination of mask supervision is a practically significant advance. However, a few concerns merit attention in a full review: (1) the "unbiased in the first-order approximation" claim is central and should be scrutinized carefully for mathematical rigor and for whether the higher-order bias meaningfully affects results in practice; (2) the evaluation appears limited to DTU and BlendedMVS, which are object-centric benchmarks with good coverage—it is unclear how the method generalizes to less constrained, larger-scale, or sparser-view settings; (3) the related work section as shown is fairly conventional and does not yet situate NeuS against concurrent efforts like VolSDF or UNISURF, which propose similar SDF-to-density conversions and would be the most direct competitors; and (4) the introduction would benefit from an explicit, concise statement of contributions and from quantitative teasers rather than relying solely on a qualitative toy example. Overall, the paper appears to tackle a well-defined problem with a principled and technically interesting solution, but the significance will hinge on the rigor of the bias analysis and the strength of comparisons against closely related concurrent work.

comments.title: Ablation study enumeration skips labels
comments.quote: (a) Naive Solution. (b) Straightforward Construction as shown in Eqn. 4. (e) Full Model.
comments.explanation: I first noticed that the list jumps from (b) to (e), with no (c) or (d) defined. I checked whether (c) and (d) might be defined earlier or in a figure caption, but in the passage they are not introduced. This is likely an OCR or labeling issue, but as presented to the reader it is confusing because later text refers only to 'naive solution' and 'direct construction' vs. 'our weight choice (e)'. A reader trying to match the three conditions against the figure's legend would be uncertain about what (c) and (d) are. Since only three conditions are actually described, using (a), (b), (c) would have been cleaner; labeling the last as (e) suggests missing entries.

The meaning is recoverable from context (the preceding sentence sets up the single-plane / tangent-plane assumption), so it does not actually mislead, but as written the sentence is grammatically incomplete. Likely OCR dropout; flagging as minor.

The sentence seems to be a malformed reference — likely intended to say that qualitative SDF prediction results are provided in the supplementary material, not tied to Figure 7. A careful reader could be momentarily confused about what Figure 7 contains versus what is deferred to supplementary. This is a minor presentation issue rather than a substantive error.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents NeuS, a neural implicit surface reconstruction method that elegantly bridges two dominant paradigms in the field: surface rendering (e.g., IDR) and volume rendering (e.g., NeRF). The core contribution—representing surfaces as the zero-level set of an SDF while using a carefully designed volume rendering scheme that is unbiased to first-order approximation—is well-motivated and addresses a genuine and important limitation of prior work, namely the inability of surface-rendering methods to handle abrupt depth changes and the difficulty of extracting clean geometry from NeRF-style density fields. The introduction is clearly written, with Fig. 1 providing an intuitive illustration of why gradient locality hampers IDR on self-occluded regions, and the claimed elimination of mask supervision is a practically significant advance. However, a few concerns merit attention in a full review: (1) the "unbiased in the first-order approximation" claim is central and should be scrutinized carefully for mathematical rigor and for whether the higher-order bias meaningfully affects results in practice; (2) the evaluation appears limited to DTU and BlendedMVS, which are object-centric benchmarks with good coverage—it is unclear how the method generalizes to less constrained, larger-scale, or sparser-view settings; (3) the related work section as shown is fairly conventional and does not yet situate NeuS against concurrent efforts like VolSDF or UNISURF, which propose similar SDF-to-density conversions and would be the most direct competitors; and (4) the introduction would benefit from an explicit, concise statement of contributions and from quantitative teasers rather than relying solely on a qualitative toy example. Overall, the paper appears to tackle a well-defined problem with a principled and technically interesting solution, but the significance will hinge on the rigor of the bias analysis and the strength of comparisons against closely related concurrent work.

comments.title: Unbiased requirement stated only as local maximum, not global
comments.quote: **Unbiased** . Given a camera ray **p** ( _t_ ), _w_ ( _t_ ) attains a locally maximal value at a surface intersection point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ), i.e. with _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0, that is, the point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ) is on the zero-level set of the SDF ( **x** ).
comments.explanation: At first reading, I wondered whether 'locally maximal' is strong enough to be called 'unbiased.' The authors' earlier motivation (Fig. 2a) contrasts the naive solution where the maximum of w(t) occurs BEFORE the surface point t*; their fix makes w(t) peak AT t*. In the single-surface case the claim is that t* is the (unique) maximum, and in the multi-surface case it is a local maximum per surface — which is the best one can ask for. So 'locally maximal' is the technically correct statement and is consistent with Theorem 1 ('attains its maximum at t*' in the interval [t_l, t_r]). The concern is only mild: the informal prose right after ('contributes most to the pixel color') slightly overstates what 'local max' guarantees, because a local max of w does not by itself imply that t* contributes the most to the integrated color — e.g., a wide low-weight region could outweigh a narrow peak. However, this is a framing concern and is effectively resolved by the occlusion-awareness property plus the convergence of 1/s → 0 which concentrates the weight. Worth noting as minor.

comments.title: Occlusion-aware condition written as f(t_0)=f(t_1) instead of using the S-density
comments.quote: Given any two depth values _t_ 0 and _t_ 1 satisfying _f_ ( _t_ 0) = _f_ ( _t_ 1), _w_ ( _t_ 0) _>_ 0, _w_ ( _t_ 1) _>_ 0, and _t_ 0 _< t_ 1, there is _w_ ( _t_ 0) _> w_ ( _t_ 1).
comments.explanation: Initial concern: f is a function of a 3D point, not of t directly. Here f(t_0) is shorthand for f(p(t_0)), which is a minor abuse of notation but standard in this paper (the authors write f(p(t)) elsewhere, so readers will infer it). More substantively, I checked whether requiring equality f(t_0)=f(t_1) is the right condition. The parenthetical 'thus the same SDF-induced S-density value' confirms the intent: because φ_s is a function of f, equal f implies equal S-density, and the condition expresses that among points with equal local density contribution, the closer one should dominate. This is the standard occlusion semantics and is fine. No real issue beyond the mild notational shorthand.

comments.title: Weight function formula missing factor T(t)
comments.quote: Recall that the weight function within the framework of volume rendering is given by _w_ ( _t_ ) = _t T_ ( _t_ ) _ρ_ ( _t_ ), where _T_ ( _t_ ) = exp( _−_ 0 _[ρ]_[(] _[u]_[)d] _[u]_[)][ denotes the] _[ accumulated transmittance]_
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered if the 't T(t) ρ(t)' is a typo or OCR artifact. The standard volume rendering weight is w(t) = T(t) ρ(t), which matches Eqn. 3 earlier in the passage (w(t) = T(t) σ(t)). The stray 't' before T(t) appears to be OCR noise rather than an author error, since Eqn. 3 above is correctly written without it. This is likely an OCR artifact per the note at the top, so I will not flag it as a real issue — but flagging for awareness that the rendered formula here reads 't T(t) ρ(t)' which would be dimensionally wrong if taken literally.

comments.title: Sign relationship between dT/dt and dΦ_s/dt appears inconsistent
comments.quote: it is easy to verify that _T_ ( _t_ ) _ρ_ ( _t_ )=− d _T_ ( _t_ )/d _t_. Further, note that |cos( _θ_ )| _φ_ _s_ ( _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ ))) = − dΦ _s_ ( _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )))/d _t_. It follows that d _T_ ( _t_ )/d _t_ = dΦ _s_ ( _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )))/d _t_.
comments.explanation: Let me work through this. For a ray with f(p(t)) = -|cos θ|(t - t*), we have df/dt = -|cos θ|. Then dΦ_s(f(p(t)))/dt = Φ_s'(f) · df/dt = φ_s(f) · (-|cos θ|) = -|cos θ| φ_s(f). So |cos θ| φ_s(f) = -dΦ_s/dt ✓. Now from the normalized S-density definition (Eqn. 4), w(t) = φ_s(f)/∫φ_s(f)du. Combined with T(t)ρ(t) = -dT/dt and w(t) = T(t)ρ(t), we need T(t)ρ(t) = φ_s(f)/∫φ_s du. For the derivation here they're showing T(t)ρ(t) = |cos θ| φ_s(f) (up to normalization) = -dΦ_s/dt. And T(t)ρ(t) = -dT/dt. So -dT/dt = -dΦ_s/dt, giving dT/dt = dΦ_s/dt, which matches. So integrating gives T(t) = Φ_s(f(p(t))) + C, consistent with the next step. The derivation is correct; my initial confusion about signs is resolved. Not an issue.

comments.title: Clipping against zero for monotonically increasing SDF segments does not fully justify unbiasedness claim
comments.quote: Thus we clip it against zero to ensure that the value of _ρ_ is always non-negative. This gives the following opaque density function _ρ_ ( _t_ ) in in general cases.
comments.explanation: The reasoning here is that on segments where f is increasing (ray exiting the surface or moving away), -dΦ_s/dt becomes negative, so ρ would be negative without clipping. Clipping to 0 on exit-segments means those segments contribute zero opacity/weight, so the ray effectively 'sees through' the back of a surface until it hits another decreasing-f segment (next surface entry). This is a design choice rather than a derivation — it's intuitive but the authors defer the formal unbiasedness proof (Theorem 1) to the supplementary. The text states the theorem covers 'general cases (i.e., including both single surface intersection and multiple surface intersections)' with the proof in supplementary. Given the forward reference to supplementary, this is not underspecified for the paper's scope. Not flagging.

comments.title: Ablation study enumeration skips labels
comments.quote: (a) Naive Solution. (b) Straightforward Construction as shown in Eqn. 4. (e) Full Model.
comments.explanation: I first noticed that the list jumps from (b) to (e), with no (c) or (d) defined. I checked whether (c) and (d) might be defined earlier or in a figure caption, but in the passage they are not introduced. This is likely an OCR or labeling issue, but as presented to the reader it is confusing because later text refers only to 'naive solution' and 'direct construction' vs. 'our weight choice (e)'. A reader trying to match the three conditions against the figure's legend would be uncertain about what (c) and (d) are. Since only three conditions are actually described, using (a), (b), (c) would have been cleaner; labeling the last as (e) suggests missing entries.

comments.title: Occlusion-aware requirement stated as f(t_0)=f(t_1) rather than f(p(t_0))=f(p(t_1))
comments.quote: Given any two depth values _t_ 0 and _t_ 1 satisfying _f_ ( _t_ 0) = _f_ ( _t_ 1)
comments.explanation: The SDF f is defined on R^3 as f: R^3 → R, so f(t_0) does not type-check — it should be f(p(t_0)) = f(p(t_1)). This is a minor notational shorthand and the intent is clear from context (the summary of requirements even restates it correctly), so it won't mislead a careful reader, but it is technically inconsistent with the formal definition of f.

comments.title: Unbiased requirement references 'the SDF (x)' with missing symbol
comments.quote: the point **p** ( _t[∗]_ ) is on the zero-level set of the SDF ( **x** ).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'the SDF (x)' appears to be missing the function symbol f; it should read 'the SDF f(x)'. This is almost certainly an OCR/typesetting artifact rather than an author error, and the meaning is unambiguous. Flagging only for completeness.

comments.title: 'Because the surface is assumed locally' appears truncated
comments.quote: Because the surface is assumed locally, _|_ cos( _θ_ ) _|_ is a constant.
comments.explanation: This sentence reads as if a word is missing — presumably 'the surface is assumed locally planar' or 'the normal is assumed locally constant'. The meaning is recoverable from context (the preceding sentence sets up the single-plane / tangent-plane assumption), so it does not actually mislead, but as written the sentence is grammatically incomplete. Likely OCR dropout; flagging as minor.

comments.title: Ambiguous figure reference in ablation discussion
comments.quote: Qualitative results of Figure 7: SDF predictions are provided in the supplementary material.
comments.explanation: Initially this reads as if Figure 7 shows SDF predictions, but Figure 7 in the passage is actually the visual comparison with UNISURF. The sentence seems to be a malformed reference — likely intended to say that qualitative SDF prediction results are provided in the supplementary material, not tied to Figure 7. A careful reader could be momentarily confused about what Figure 7 contains versus what is deferred to supplementary. This is a minor presentation issue rather than a substantive error.

comments.title: Ablation claim that Chamfer is 'on par' contradicts reported numbers
comments.quote: Without Eikonal regularization or geometric initialization, the result on Chamfer distance is on par with that of the full model.
comments.explanation: The text claims that removing Eikonal regularization or geometric initialization yields Chamfer distance on par with the full model. Looking at the reported numbers in Figure 6's caption text: w/o Eikonal = 0.64, w/o Geo-Init = 0.62, Full Model = 0.59. These are indeed close (within ~10%), so 'on par' is defensible. However, the MAE values (88.94 and 6.19 vs 0.93) are dramatically worse, which the authors do correctly highlight. The claim is consistent with the numerics for Chamfer specifically, so this is not actually an error — the authors' point is that Chamfer alone doesn't reveal the SDF-quality problem. No issue.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and well-motivated contribution: combining the robustness of volume rendering with the geometric precision of SDF-based implicit surfaces addresses a clear limitation in prior neural reconstruction methods such as IDR and NeRF. The abstract and introduction are generally clear, logically structured, and effective in explaining the central technical insight—namely that naïvely applying volume rendering to an SDF-induced density introduces geometric bias, motivating a new unbiased formulation. The positioning relative to existing work is convincing, and the illustrative comparison in Figure 1 helps communicate why single-surface rendering struggles with occlusion and abrupt depth changes. The main issue at this stage is that some claims are quite strong—e.g., “free of bias in the first order” and reconstruction “without mask supervision”—but the excerpt has not yet provided enough intuition or formal detail to make these claims fully assessable; the paper will need a rigorous derivation and careful ablations to substantiate them. The writing is mostly polished, though there are minor grammatical issues and occasional overstatements, and the distinction between limitations of NeRF’s density field and the proposed SDF-density conversion could be sharpened for readers less familiar with volume rendering. Overall, the beginning suggests a high-quality and impactful paper with a clear problem formulation, but its ultimate strength depends on the mathematical justification of the proposed rendering scheme and the fairness and breadth of the empirical comparisons.

comments.title: Related-work grouping incorrectly implies UNISURF uses surface rendering
comments.quote: Recent works represent surfaces as signed distance functions (SDF) [46, 49, 17, 22] or occupancy [29, 30]. To train their neural models, these methods use a differentiable surface rendering method to render a 3D object into images and compare them against input images for supervision.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a broad setup for contrasting surface-rendering methods with the authors' volume-rendering approach, which is a reasonable introductory simplification for methods such as IDR. However, the citation list appears to include [30], which the paper later identifies as UNISURF, a concurrent method that learns an implicit surface via volume rendering rather than a purely differentiable surface-rendering formulation. The later Related Works section partly resolves the authors' intent by separately discussing UNISURF as a volume-rendering-based method, but that makes this earlier blanket statement technically inaccurate. A reader following the citations could reasonably conclude that all cited occupancy/SDF surface methods, including UNISURF, use surface rendering, which contradicts the later taxonomy.

comments.title: Volume rendering gradient support is overstated
comments.quote: The advantage of the volume rendering approach is that it can handle abrupt depth changes, because it considers multiple points along the ray and so all the sample points, either near the surface or on the far surface, produce gradient signals for back propagation.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with surface rendering is clear and largely correct: alpha-composited volume rendering can distribute supervision over multiple samples along a ray, which often gives a broader optimization basin than relying on a single ray-surface intersection. However, the phrase "all the sample points" is too strong under the standard NeRF rendering equations. Gradients to a sample's color are weighted by its rendering weight, typically T_i alpha_i, and gradients to density can also be very small or effectively zero when transmittance is exhausted by nearer density, when alpha is near zero, or when samples lie in irrelevant empty regions. Thus far-surface samples behind an already opaque near surface do not necessarily receive meaningful gradient. The surrounding context makes the qualitative point understandable, but the literal claim overstates what volume rendering guarantees.

comments.title: Logistic density scale and standard deviation are described inaccurately
comments.quote:
Note that the standard deviation of _φs_ ( _x_ ) is given by 1 _/s_ , which is also a trainable parameter, that is, 1 _/s_ approaches to zero as the network training converges.

Moreover, NeuS has only a single scale parameter _s_ that is used to model the standard deviation of the probability distribution for all the spatial location.
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly using \(s\) as a learned sharpness parameter for the logistic density \(\phi_s(x)=s e^{-sx}/(1+e^{-sx})^2\), and the qualitative point is right: increasing \(s\) concentrates the density around the zero level set. However, for a logistic distribution with scale parameter \(1/s\), the standard deviation is \(\pi/(\sqrt{3}s)\), not \(1/s\), and certainly not \(s\) itself. Thus \(1/s\) is the logistic scale parameter, or proportional to the standard deviation, while \(s\) is an inverse-scale or sharpness parameter. The statement that \(1/s\) approaches zero as training converges is also presented as if guaranteed, whereas no optimization argument or constraint establishes this; at most it is an intended or empirical behavior. This constant-factor issue probably does not affect the method or implementation, but the terminology is mathematically imprecise.

comments.title: Normalization constant is dropped in the derivation of the opaque density
comments.quote: Specifically, in the simple case of a single plane intersection, it is easy to see that the signed distance function _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )) is _−|_ cos( _θ_ ) _| ·_ ( _t − t[∗]_ ), where _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0, and _θ_ is the angle between the view direction **v** and the outward surface normal vector **n** . Because the surface is assumed locally, _|_ cos( _θ_ ) _|_ is a constant. It follows from Eqn. 4 that
comments.explanation: The intended derivation is recognizable: for a locally planar surface with f(p(t)) = -a(t-t*) where a=|cos θ|, the normalized S-density along the ray should be proportional to a φ_s(f(p(t))), and this motivates ρ(t) = -d log Φ_s(f(p(t)))/dt. However, if Eqn. 4 normalizes over the actual ray interval [0,∞), then ∫_0^∞ φ_s(f(p(u)))du = Φ_s(f(p(0)))/a, not exactly 1/a, unless one assumes Φ_s(f(p(0)))≈1 or starts the ray at an ideal point infinitely far outside the object. Equivalently, the transmittance satisfying T(0)=1 should be T(t)=Φ_s(f(p(t)))/Φ_s(f(p(0))) in the single-plane case, not simply T(t)=Φ_s(f(p(t))). The multiplicative constant cancels when taking -T'/T, so the final continuous formula for ρ is likely still the authors' intended one. But as written, the derivation silently uses an approximation or boundary assumption, which is nontrivial because T(0)=1 is a standard volume-rendering convention.

comments.title: Weight-function guarantees are stated more broadly than the derivation proves
comments.quote:
To this end, now we shall design the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) that is both occlusion-aware and unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF, based on the aforementioned straightforward construction. To ensure an occlusion-aware property of the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ), we will still follow the basic framework of volume rendering as Eqn. 3.

The following theorem states that in general cases (i.e., including both single surface intersection and multiple surface intersections) the weight function defined by Eqn. 10 and Eqn. 5 is unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF.
comments.explanation: The construction is qualitatively occlusion-aware because standard volume rendering multiplies local opacity/density by monotonically decreasing transmittance, so nearer surfaces tend to dominate farther ones. However, the paper's earlier formal occlusion-aware condition is stronger: for any t0<t1 with f(t0)=f(t1) and positive weights, one should have w(t0)>w(t1). For the proposed opaque density, ρ(t) also depends on the ray derivative of the SDF, essentially through -df/dt. Thus two points with the same SDF value can have very different local densities because of incidence angle or local geometry, and a farther point with much larger -df/dt could in principle receive a larger weight than a nearer grazing point. Separately, the theorem's unbiasedness result assumes a locally decreasing SDF as the ray enters the surface from outside to inside. Multiple-intersection rays also contain inside-to-outside crossings where f increases, and the proposed clipping can set the density to zero there, so the result does not establish unbiasedness at every zero-level crossing. The claims would be safer if scoped to visible outside-to-inside intersections under the theorem's local assumptions, rather than to general multiple-surface cases or to the full formal occlusion-aware ordering.

comments.title: Ground-truth SDF MAE is underspecified
comments.quote: The MAE is computed on uniformly-sampled points in the object’s bounding sphere.
comments.explanation: I initially read the MAE as a useful complement to Chamfer distance: it is reasonable to show that an ablated model can recover a similar zero level set while failing to learn a metric signed distance field. However, computing an MAE against “ground-truth SDF” is not a standard dataset-independent operation in the same way that Chamfer distance to a ground-truth point cloud is. A reader needs to know how the ground-truth SDF was obtained: whether it was computed from a watertight mesh, how signs were assigned, whether distances were normalized or truncated, what coordinate scale was used, and how points outside the observed object or near open boundaries were handled. Uniform sampling in the bounding sphere also makes the metric sensitive to far-field values, so the reported MAE values can be dominated by regions away from the surface. The qualitative conclusion may be correct, but the evaluation protocol is not sufficiently specified here to make the MAE numbers reproducible or fully interpretable.

comments.title: State-of-the-art claim is broader than the evidence stated here
comments.quote: It outperforms the state-of-the-arts both qualitatively and quantitatively.
comments.explanation: The paper does present qualitative and quantitative comparisons, and in context the claim is meant relative to the evaluated baselines such as IDR, NeRF, COLMAP, and UNISURF on the reported datasets. Still, the conclusion states the result in a very broad form: “the state-of-the-arts” without scoping it to the benchmark datasets, metrics, reconstruction settings, and compared methods. Since surface reconstruction performance can vary substantially with object class, camera setup, mask availability, texture, and evaluation protocol, the claim would be more defensible if explicitly limited to the experiments conducted in the paper.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and well-motivated contribution: combining the robustness of volume rendering with the geometric precision of SDF-based implicit surfaces addresses a clear limitation in prior neural reconstruction methods such as IDR and NeRF. The abstract and introduction are generally clear, logically structured, and effective in explaining the central technical insight—namely that naïvely applying volume rendering to an SDF-induced density introduces geometric bias, motivating a new unbiased formulation. The positioning relative to existing work is convincing, and the illustrative comparison in Figure 1 helps communicate why single-surface rendering struggles with occlusion and abrupt depth changes. The main issue at this stage is that some claims are quite strong—e.g., “free of bias in the first order” and reconstruction “without mask supervision”—but the excerpt has not yet provided enough intuition or formal detail to make these claims fully assessable; the paper will need a rigorous derivation and careful ablations to substantiate them. The writing is mostly polished, though there are minor grammatical issues and occasional overstatements, and the distinction between limitations of NeRF’s density field and the proposed SDF-density conversion could be sharpened for readers less familiar with volume rendering. Overall, the beginning suggests a high-quality and impactful paper with a clear problem formulation, but its ultimate strength depends on the mathematical justification of the proposed rendering scheme and the fairness and breadth of the empirical comparisons.

comments.title: Related-work grouping incorrectly implies UNISURF uses surface rendering
comments.quote: Recent works represent surfaces as signed distance functions (SDF) [46, 49, 17, 22] or occupancy [29, 30]. To train their neural models, these methods use a differentiable surface rendering method to render a 3D object into images and compare them against input images for supervision.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a broad setup for contrasting surface-rendering methods with the authors' volume-rendering approach, which is a reasonable introductory simplification for methods such as IDR. However, the citation list appears to include [30], which the paper later identifies as UNISURF, a concurrent method that learns an implicit surface via volume rendering rather than a purely differentiable surface-rendering formulation. The later Related Works section partly resolves the authors' intent by separately discussing UNISURF as a volume-rendering-based method, but that makes this earlier blanket statement technically inaccurate. A reader following the citations could reasonably conclude that all cited occupancy/SDF surface methods, including UNISURF, use surface rendering, which contradicts the later taxonomy.

comments.title: Volume rendering gradient support is overstated
comments.quote: The advantage of the volume rendering approach is that it can handle abrupt depth changes, because it considers multiple points along the ray and so all the sample points, either near the surface or on the far surface, produce gradient signals for back propagation.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with surface rendering is clear and largely correct: alpha-composited volume rendering can distribute supervision over multiple samples along a ray, which often gives a broader optimization basin than relying on a single ray-surface intersection. However, the phrase "all the sample points" is too strong under the standard NeRF rendering equations. comments.title: Logistic density standard deviation is misstated
comments.quote: Note that the standard deviation of _φs_ ( _x_ ) is given by 1 _/s_ , which is also a trainable parameter, that is, 1 _/s_ approaches to zero as the network training converges.
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly using \(s\) as a learned sharpness parameter for the logistic density \(\phi_s(x)=s e^{-sx}/(1+e^{-sx})^2\), and the qualitative point is right: increasing \(s\) concentrates the density around the zero level set. However, for a logistic distribution with scale parameter \(1/s\), the standard deviation is \(\pi/(\sqrt{3}s)\), not \(1/s\). Thus \(1/s\) is the logistic scale parameter, or proportional to the standard deviation, rather than the standard deviation itself. This constant factor probably does not affect the method or implementation, but the statement is mathematically inaccurate. The additional claim that \(1/s\) approaches zero as training converges is also presented as if guaranteed, whereas in the passage no optimization argument or constraint has yet established this; at most it is an intended or empirical behavior.

comments.title: Calling \(\phi_s(f(x))\) a probability density function is imprecise
comments.quote: In order to apply a volume rendering method to training the SDF network, we first introduce a probability density function _φs_ ( _f_ ( **x** )), called _S-density_ , where _f_ ( **x** ), **x** _∈_ R[3] , is the signed distance
comments.explanation: I understand the intended construction: \(\phi_s\) is the one-dimensional logistic density over signed-distance values, and composing it with the SDF gives a scalar field that is large near the surface. That is a reasonable device for connecting an SDF to volume-rendering weights. The potentially misleading point is the phrase "probability density function \(\phi_s(f(x))\)". While \(\phi_s(u)\) is a normalized density with respect to the scalar variable \(u\), the composed field \(\phi_s(f(x))\) is not, by itself, a probability density over 3D space or even along a ray unless one accounts for the geometry/Jacobian and normalization. Later parts of the method derive separate rendering weights, so the issue is mostly terminological, but a careful reader could initially infer a stronger probabilistic meaning than is actually justified here.

comments.title: Normalization constant is dropped in the derivation of the opaque density
comments.quote: Specifically, in the simple case of a single plane intersection, it is easy to see that the signed distance function _f_ ( **p** ( _t_ )) is _−|_ cos( _θ_ ) _| ·_ ( _t − t[∗]_ ), where _f_ ( **p** ( _t[∗]_ )) = 0, and _θ_ is the angle between the view direction **v** and the outward surface normal vector **n** . Because the surface is assumed locally, _|_ cos( _θ_ ) _|_ is a constant. It follows from Eqn. 4 that
comments.explanation: The intended derivation is recognizable: for a locally planar surface with f(p(t)) = -a(t-t*) where a=|cos θ|, the normalized S-density along the ray should be proportional to a φ_s(f(p(t))), and this motivates ρ(t) = -d log Φ_s(f(p(t)))/dt. However, if Eqn. 4 normalizes over the actual ray interval [0,∞), then ∫_0^∞ φ_s(f(p(u)))du = Φ_s(f(p(0)))/a, not exactly 1/a, unless one assumes Φ_s(f(p(0)))≈1 or starts the ray at an ideal point infinitely far outside the object. Equivalently, the transmittance satisfying T(0)=1 should be T(t)=Φ_s(f(p(t)))/Φ_s(f(p(0))) in the single-plane case, not simply T(t)=Φ_s(f(p(t))). The multiplicative constant cancels when taking -T'/T, so the final continuous formula for ρ is likely still the authors' intended one. But as written, the derivation silently uses an approximation or boundary assumption, which is nontrivial because T(0)=1 is a standard volume-rendering convention.

comments.title: Occlusion-aware property is asserted but not guaranteed by transmittance alone
comments.quote: To this end, now we shall design the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ) that is both occlusion-aware and unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF, based on the aforementioned straightforward construction. To ensure an occlusion-aware property of the weight function _w_ ( _t_ ), we will still follow the basic framework of volume rendering as Eqn. 3.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended intuition: standard volume rendering multiplies the local opacity/density by a monotonically decreasing transmittance, so nearer surfaces tend to dominate farther ones. This does make the construction qualitatively occlusion-aware. However, the formal occlusion-aware requirement stated earlier is stronger: for any t0<t1 with f(t0)=f(t1) and positive weights, one should have w(t0)>w(t1). For the naive σ(t)=φ_s(f(p(t))) construction this follows because the local density is identical at equal SDF values and only T changes. For the proposed opaque density, though, ρ(t) also depends on the ray derivative of the SDF, essentially through -dΦ_s(f(p(t)))/dt, i.e. through -df/dt. Thus two points with the same SDF value can have very different local ρ values because of different incidence angles or local geometry. A farther point with much larger -df/dt could in principle have T(t1)ρ(t1) exceeding T(t0)ρ(t0), especially if the nearer crossing is grazing. The theorem that follows establishes unbiasedness under local assumptions, but it does not prove the stated occlusion-aware ordering. The claim would be safer if qualified as an occlusion-handling volume-rendering construction rather than as satisfying the earlier formal occlusion-aware condition without additional assumptions.

comments.title: The claimed general unbiasedness is broader than the theorem’s actual assumptions
comments.quote:
The following theorem states that in general cases (i.e.,

including both single surface intersection and multiple surface intersections) the weight function defined by Eqn. 10 and Eqn. 5 is unbiased in the first-order approximation of SDF.
comments.explanation: The theorem is useful and matches the construction’s intended behavior near a visible front-facing crossing: it assumes the ray enters the surface from outside to inside and that f(p(t)) is monotonically decreasing on a local interval. Under those assumptions, clipping the positive-density part and using volume rendering can indeed yield a local maximum at the outside-to-inside zero crossing. What remains potentially misleading is the preceding phrase 'in general cases,' especially because multiple surface intersections also include inside-to-outside crossings where f is increasing. In those intervals the proposed construction clips the density to zero, so the weight will not generally have a local maximum at that zero-level crossing. This is probably intentional for visibility/occlusion reasons, but it means the unbiasedness statement only applies to locally decreasing, visible entering intersections, not to arbitrary surface intersections in a multi-intersection ray. A careful reader could otherwise think the theorem proves unbiasedness at every zero of the SDF along the ray.

comments.title: Ground-truth SDF MAE is underspecified
comments.quote: The MAE is computed on uniformly-sampled points in the object’s bounding sphere.
comments.explanation: I initially read the MAE as a useful complement to Chamfer distance: it is reasonable to show that an ablated model can recover a similar zero level set while failing to learn a metric signed distance field. However, computing an MAE against “ground-truth SDF” is not a standard dataset-independent operation in the same way that Chamfer distance to a ground-truth point cloud is. A reader needs to know how the ground-truth SDF was obtained: whether it was computed from a watertight mesh, how signs were assigned, whether distances were normalized or truncated, what coordinate scale was used, and how points outside the observed object or near open boundaries were handled. Uniform sampling in the bounding sphere also makes the metric sensitive to far-field values, so the reported MAE values can be dominated by regions away from the surface. The qualitative conclusion may be correct, but the evaluation protocol is not sufficiently specified here to make the MAE numbers reproducible or fully interpretable.

comments.title: The role of the global scale parameter \(s\) is described imprecisely
comments.quote: Moreover, NeuS has only a single scale parameter _s_ that is used to model the standard deviation of the probability distribution for all the spatial location.
comments.explanation: The intended point is clear and reasonable: NeuS uses one global sharpness/scale parameter for the SDF-induced logistic density, so a spatially varying uncertainty or thickness model could be a useful extension. However, with the earlier definition \(\Phi_s(x)=(1+e^{-sx})^{-1}\), the associated logistic distribution has scale \(1/s\) and standard deviation \(\pi/(\sqrt{3}s)\), not literally \(s\) itself. Also, the distribution is over SDF values, while its spatial spread depends on the SDF geometry and gradient behavior; saying it models the standard deviation “for all the spatial location” could make readers think a spatial Gaussian-like variance is explicitly defined everywhere. This is mostly a wording issue—the authors are using \(s\) as a global sharpness parameter—but the statement would be technically clearer if phrased as a single global inverse-scale/sharpness parameter controlling the spread of the S-density.

comments.title: State-of-the-art claim is broader than the evidence stated here
comments.quote: It outperforms the state-of-the-arts both qualitatively and quantitatively.
comments.explanation: The paper does present qualitative and quantitative comparisons, and in context the claim is meant relative to the evaluated baselines such as IDR, NeRF, COLMAP, and UNISURF on the reported datasets. Still, the conclusion states the result in a very broad form: “the state-of-the-arts” without scoping it to the benchmark datasets, metrics, reconstruction settings, and compared methods. Since surface reconstruction performance can vary substantially with object class, camera setup, mask availability, texture, and evaluation protocol, the claim would be more defensible if explicitly limited to the experiments conducted in the paper.
